method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Tromelin Island is named in honour of Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine, who reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1] The island was formerly known as the Isle of Sand, reflecting its low, flat, sandy character.[1][4] Its French name is Île Tromelin, while the English form is Tromelin Island.[1][5]

==Description==
Tromelin Island lies in the Indian Ocean in the Mascarene Basin, about 500 km north of Réunion and about 450 km east of Madagascar.[1] It is part of the Îles Éparses, administered by France within the French Southern and Antarctic Lands, while Mauritius also claims sovereignty over it.[1] The island is low, flat and sandy, with a maximum elevation of about 7 m above sea level.[1][3] It is thought to be the eroded remnant of a volcanic seamount that developed a coral atoll ring.[1][3] Tromelin is uninhabited apart from scientific visits and a small research and weather-station presence.[1][3][4] The island is an important nesting site for seabirds and green sea turtles, and its seabird populations have benefited from the eradication of invasive rodents.[1][4]

==Fauna and flora==
The island’s terrestrial fauna is dominated by seabirds, while green sea turtles are the main non-avian vertebrates known to nest there; no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians occur.[1] Before the impact of introduced Norway rats, Tromelin supported an estimated six to eight breeding seabird species, but by 2005 only two booby species remained and breeding success was limited.[1] Following rat eradication in 2005, the seabird community recovered naturally, reaching seven breeding species and increasing from 353 to 4,758 breeding pairs by 2022.[1] The masked booby, represented locally by the endemic subspecies Sula dactylatra melanops, is among the principal breeding seabirds recorded on the island.[1] Surveys between 2017 and 2021 also recorded eleven non-breeding seabird species, with great and lesser frigatebirds using the island regularly as a roosting site and tropicbirds occasionally landing or displaying.[5] Mice are now the only introduced mammals reported as present on the island.[5]

The vegetation is sparse and low-growing, reflecting exposure to wind, salt spray and limited freshwater availability.[1] The native flora was historically simple, centred on the littoral shrub Tournefortia argentea, also known as Heliotropium foertherianum or tree heliotrope, together with fewer than ten herbaceous species and no native trees.[1] Norway rats suppressed native plants by consuming seeds, seedlings and foliage, but monitoring after eradication recorded a recovery to seven native taxa by 2013, including increased density and height of Tournefortia argentea.[1] A small number of introduced coconut palms persist, although they do not dominate the native plant assemblage.[1] Conservation work on the island focuses on preventing further invasive-species introductions so that the recovery of native vegetation and seabird habitat can continue.[1]

==History==
French accounts identify the island’s European discovery in the 1720s, when the navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée recorded it and named it Île de Sable, meaning “Isle of Sand”.[4] The island later became associated with the name Tromelin, while geographic-name authorities record both the French form Île Tromelin and the English form Tromelin Island.[2][4]

One of the best-known episodes in the island’s history is the wreck of the ship Utile, which is treated as a distinct historical event in accounts of Tromelin.[4] The memory of this episode has continued to carry cultural and political significance in France, including press discussion of why Tromelin provokes strong reactions and why the history connected with the island should be preserved.[5]

France traces its sovereignty claim to 29 November 1776, the date on which the ship Dauphine arrived at the island.[4] Mauritius bases its rival claim on the argument that Tromelin should have been ceded to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and therefore should not have remained administered by France as a dependency of Réunion.[4] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[4] France built a meteorological station and a landing strip on the island in 1954, strengthening its continuing administrative presence there.[4]

Modern geographic and administrative databases list Tromelin as a French possession and as administratively part of France, while also associating it with Réunion in the territorial hierarchy.[2] The sovereignty dispute nevertheless remains part of the island’s political history, with Mauritius continuing to assert its claim despite French administration.[1][4]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate under the Köppen classification, with consistently warm conditions and a distinct wet season from November to April associated with the intertropical convergence zone.[3] The mean annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, with little seasonal variation owing to the island’s tropical oceanic setting.[3] Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, with rainfall concentrated in the summer months; February is typically the wettest month, while May to October is comparatively dry.[3] Prevailing south-easterly trade winds dominate the island’s weather, commonly blowing from directions between about 100° and 140° and often exceeding 8 m/s.[3] The island is low, flat and sandy, a physical setting that leaves little topographic shelter from wind and marine weather conditions.[5] A meteorological station has operated on Tromelin since 1949, monitoring tropical cyclones and contributing to regional weather forecasting in the south-west Indian Ocean.[3]
